topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with a stark, unforgiving subject line: "Important Notice Regarding [College Name]." I clicked it open, my thumb hovering over the screen, rereading the text multiple times. My heart gave a strange, uneven thump against my ribs. It wasn't a prank; the language was official, heavy with finality. I leaned back in my worn desk chair, the cheap plastic groaning in protest under my weight. For a full minute, I just stared at the screen, the fluorescent lights of my tiny dorm room buzzing overhead seeming suddenly too loud. Then, a slow, creeping cold started in my stomach, and I pushed the laptop away, needing to stand up, needing air that felt suddenly too thin.

[story 2]

"Wait, what?" I managed to croak out, the words feeling thick and unfamiliar in my own mouth. Professor Albright’s announcement had been delivered in the lecture hall, calm and measured, but when the details started filtering through the campus news feed—the liquidation of the main academic wing, the shuttering of the library—my jaw actually dropped. I gripped the edge of the table so tightly my knuckles turned white. I started pacing the small confines of my apartment, my footsteps echoing hollowly on the linoleum floor. Every familiar corner of the campus map suddenly looked menacing, the vibrant murals seeming to mock the news I was absorbing. I stopped abruptly by the window, pulling the curtain aside, just wanting to see something solid that wasn't dissolving.

[story 3]

I was sorting through old syllabi, a mundane task usually providing a comforting rhythm to my days, when I found the memo tucked beneath a stack of outdated course catalogs. My eyes scanned the dense paragraph detailing the administrative restructuring, and then they snagged on the final sentence. A silence descended around me, heavy and absolute. I didn't move for a long time. My breath hitched, shallow and rapid. I let the papers slip from my fingers, and they fluttered to the floor like dead leaves. A strange lightness washed over me, quickly replaced by a dizzying sense of disorientation. I stood up, walked to the window overlooking the quad, and just watched the familiar bustle—the students laughing, the cars passing—as if observing a scene from a distant, unreal film.
